evidence_quotes.quote: Games shouldn't be killed just because a publisher wants to push newer titles or sunset legacy systems.
Consumers are often led to believe they're buying a product, when in reality theyre only granted temporary access through fragile licensing systems.
Clear disclosure of DRM dependencies, license terms, and conditions that may lead to future inaccessibility.

paragraphs.text: As a supporter of the Stop Killing Games movement, I want to express my concern about the increasing number of digital products, especially video games, rendered unplayable due to developer decisions or DRM restrictions. Consumers are often led to believe they're buying a product, when in reality theyre only granted temporary access through fragile licensing systems. It is troubling that many games now rely heavily on authentication servers that can be taken offline without warning. This leaves players with unusable software, despite having paid full price. Such tactics constitute a form of planned obsolescence, denying consumers lasting value and ownership. To support a truly fair digital market, I believe the Act should require: Developers to take into consideration and prepare for end-of-life plans during development. Clear disclosure of DRM dependencies, license terms, and conditions that may lead to future inaccessibility. Legal protection for consumers to access and preserve in some form purchased content, especially when it is technically feasible to do so offline or through alternative methods. This isn't a request for perpetual developer support. Its a plea to recognize ownership and empower consumers to preserve the software theyve paid for. Games shouldn't be killed just because a publisher wants to push newer titles or sunset legacy systems. Thank you for opening this avenue for public feedback. I hope these concerns are considered seriously, as they reflect a growing frustration among digital consumers and gamers across Europe.
